title: What is Image Noise?
concept: Image noise is random, unwanted variation in pixel values that doesn't come from the real scene — usually introduced by low light or the limits of the camera sensor.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does an image filter (kernel) do?

options.text: Slides across the image and recalculates each pixel from its neighbors
Compresses the image into a smaller file size

exercises.explain: Right! Filters recompute pixels from their neighborhood — today we look at a problem those filters are often used to fix: noise.

cards.title: Why night photos look speckly
cards.body: Take a photo in bright daylight and it looks clean and smooth. Take one in a dim room, and suddenly it's covered in tiny random specks that weren't part of the scene at all. That speckling is image noise.

cards.title: What is image noise?
cards.body: Image noise is random, unwanted variation in pixel values that doesn't correspond to anything really in the scene. A patch of plain gray wall should be one steady color, but noise makes individual pixels randomly flicker slightly brighter or darker than they should be.

cards.title: Static on an old radio
cards.body: Think of static hissing underneath a radio broadcast. The music is the real signal; the hiss is random noise layered on top, coming from the equipment itself, not the song. Image noise works the same way — it's random fuzz sitting on top of the real picture.

cards.title: Same scene, two different lights
cards.body: Photograph a plain wall in bright sunlight and every pixel comes out nearly identical. Photograph the same wall in a dim room and the pixels jitter randomly around the true color — some slightly brighter, some slightly darker — even though the wall itself never changed.

cards.title: Noise isn't extra detail
cards.body: It's tempting to think a grainy photo has "more information" in it. It doesn't. Noise is random measurement error, not real structure — it changes every time you take the shot, while the actual scene stays the same.

cards.title: Bright light vs. low light capture
cards.body: Why noise shows up more in the dark:

left.text:
Sensor captures a strong, clean signal
Little to no visible noise

right.text:
Sensor struggles to capture a weak signal
Random noise becomes obvious

options.text: Random, unwanted variation in pixel values that isn't part of the real scene
The intentional pattern in a photo, like a striped shirt
The color palette used by the camera
A deliberate artistic effect added in editing software

exercises.explain: Correct — noise is random variation that doesn't reflect anything really there in the scene.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A photo of a plain, evenly lit wall should show every pixel as the exact same color if there's zero noise.
exercises.explain: True. With no noise, a uniform surface under even lighting would produce identical pixel values — any variation you see is noise.

exercises.sentence: Image noise is a common problem in photos taken in ___ light.
exercises.explain: Low is correct — dim lighting is one of the most common causes of visible image noise.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best illustrates image noise?

options.text: A nighttime photo where a plain dark sky shows random speckles of slightly different brightness
A photo where the sky is a perfectly smooth, uniform blue
A photo that has been resized to a smaller resolution

exercises.explain: Random speckling on what should be a uniform area is the classic sign of image noise.

exercises.prompt: A security camera captures clear video during the day but grainy, speckled footage every night. What's the most likely explanation?

options.text: Low light makes the sensor's random noise much more visible relative to the weak real signal
The camera automatically adds noise on purpose at night to save storage space
Nighttime footage always has more real detail than daytime footage
The camera's file format changes between day and night

exercises.explain: Exactly — in low light the real signal is weak, so the sensor's random noise becomes much more noticeable. Next: not all noise looks the same — let's meet its two most common flavors.
